## Security Decisions

### SAST tool selected
Explain choice: I chose CodeQL as my SAST tool because when comparing CodeQL with other options like SonarQube, I realized that SonarQube would require setting up and maintaining a separate server along with managing authentication keys. Therefore, for the sake of simplicity, I decided to use CodeQL instead. 

### SCA severity threshold selected
Explain rationale:
I selected the CRITICAL,HIGH severity threshold because, after comparing different SCA scanning approaches, I realized that lowering the threshold (for example, including Medium or Low findings) would introduce a lot of noise into the pipeline and cause unnecessary build failures. For the sake of simplicity and reliability, I decided to enforce only High‑impact vulnerabilities. These are the issues that pose an immediate security risk, such as remote code execution, authentication bypass, or supply‑chain compromise, and therefore must be blocked before merging. Additionally, Trivy already provides built‑in severity filtering, so all I needed to do was specify severity: "CRITICAL,HIGH" in the workflow. This allowed me to enforce a meaningful security gate without adding extra configuration or custom logic. The result is a clean, predictable SCA step that focuses on the vulnerabilities that matter most while keeping the pipeline maintainable.

### Security gate policy
Explain rationale:
I configured the security gate to fail the build whenever either CodeQL or Trivy reports High‑severity issues because, after comparing different enforcement approaches, I realized that relying on each tool’s built‑in gating mechanism was the simplest and most reliable option. For SAST, I scripted CodeQL to analyze the sarif file and fail if any errors with a severity >=7 were detected, which allowed me to block serious vulnerabilities, such as injection flaws and insecure authentication logic. Similarly, in the SCA pipeline, Trivy includes first‑class support for both dependency vulnerability scanning and license compliance checks through scanners: vuln, license, so all I needed to do was set severity: CRITICAL,HIGH and enable exit-code: 1 to enforce a meaningful supply‑chain quality gate. Together, these configurations ensure that dangerous code and unsafe dependencies cannot be merged into the main branch, while avoiding unnecessary noise from lower‑severity findings, resulting in a clean, predictable, and maintainable security gate across both SAST and SCA stages.

### One supply-chain risk observed
Describe:
One supply‑chain risk I observed in this project was the reliance on third‑party npm dependencies inside the vulnerable-app, which can silently introduce high or critical vulnerabilities through outdated packages or risky transitive dependencies. When comparing different ways to detect this type of risk, I realized that manually reviewing dependency trees or adding additional tooling would introduce unnecessary overhead. Therefore, for the sake of simplicity, I decided to use Trivy, since it already provides built‑in support for both dependency vulnerability scanning and license checks through scanners: vuln, license. All I needed to do was point the scanner at ./vulnerable-app and enable severity: CRITICAL,HIGH to surface issues such as vulnerable versions of express, lodash, or jsonwebtoken, with a custom config file created for dependency policies and license policies. This allowed me to identify a clear supply‑chain exposure, unsafe upstream packages inherited by the application without requiring any extra configuration beyond what Trivy already provides
